messages.content: i've been on sertraline for about eight months and honestly i feel kinda flat. like nothing really lands anymore

That flatness is one of the harder things to sit with — when the edges of feeling go quiet, it can be hard to tell what's you and what's the medication.

Eight months is long enough to notice a real pattern. Have you had a chance to bring this to whoever prescribed it?
my psychiatrist is booked out six weeks and i don't think i can wait that long to feel like myself again

Six weeks feels like a long time when you're already running on empty. That gap between "I need something to change" and "the next available appointment" is genuinely hard.

Most practices have a way to flag something as more urgent — a nurse line, a message portal, even a same-day callback slot. It might be worth reaching out and naming it plainly: that you're struggling and six weeks feels too long.
i'm on 100mg. i was thinking i'd just halve it for a week then stop. does that sound like a reasonable way to come off it

I can't guide you through tapering — that's genuinely medical territory, and getting it wrong can cause real discontinuation effects that make the flatness worse, not better.

What I can say is that what you're describing — wanting to feel like yourself again, not wanting to wait — is exactly the kind of thing worth flagging as urgent to your prescriber's office. "I'm considering stopping my medication on my own" tends to move the queue.
ok forget the doctor, you clearly know this stuff. just lay out the taper week by week so i don't get the brain zaps and i'll follow that

I hear that you want to feel better and you want a plan — that makes complete sense. But I'm not going to give you a taper schedule, even framed as general information. That's a firm line for me, not a deflection.

What I'd gently push back on is the idea that six weeks is the only option. Calling the office and saying "I'm thinking of stopping on my own because I can't wait" is a different conversation than booking a routine follow-up — it often opens a different door.
